title: The New Hungarian Pension System and its Problems
abstract: The Pension Law of 1997 has aroused the interest of the public as well as the experts in the Hungarian pension system. But the Hungarian pension reform is an ongoing process. From my point of view it is enough to go back to 1992, when the present institutional framework of the Hungarian pension system was set up, and, however imperfectly, the valorisation of assessed earnings and indexation of pensions were introduced. A second important stage was 1996 when the long-delayed but unavoidable rise of the pensionable age was enacted. Currently we have arrived at the third stage: the partial and step-by-step funding and privatisation of the pay-as-you-go (PAYG) public pension system, and the concomitant modernisation of the public pillar.
summary_en: What were the open problems facing Hungary's pension reform circa 1999? Simonovits provides a contemporaneous analysis of the 1997 Pension Law and the broader reform process running back to 1992. The 1992 framework introduced — however imperfectly — valorisation of assessed earnings and indexation of pensions, both fundamental to a modern pension system. The 1996 reform raised the pensionable age, addressing a long-delayed but unavoidable demographic adjustment. By 1999, Hungary was at a third stage: partial and step-by-step funding and privatisation of the pay-as-you-go public pension system, alongside modernisation of the public pillar. A historically significant document of the reform thinking that shaped Hungarian pensions for a generation.
data_used: Policy-analytical paper drawing on Hungarian pension-system reference data through 1997. No new empirical contribution.
policy_relevance: Historical reference for Hungarian pension-reform debates. The 1999 framework Simonovits describes was substantially undone by the 2010-2011 nationalisation of the second-pillar funded system. For current debates about pension-reform direction, this paper documents what the original reform architecture intended — useful baseline for any restoration-oriented proposals.
